title: Compare Java EE and Spring in Code

description.task: Write a Java program that demonstrates Dependency Injection using a Spring-style manual approach. Create an interface and two implementations, then manually inject a dependency into a service class.
instructions: Implement EmailService and SmsService with the getMessage() method.
Add a constructor to NotificationService that accepts a MessageService parameter.
Add a notify() method to NotificationService that prints the result of messageService.getMessage().
In Main, create NotificationService twice — once with EmailService and once with SmsService.
description.hint: This is the core idea behind both Java EE CDI and Spring DI: the consumer does not create its dependencies; they are provided from outside.
description.expectedOutput:
Email message sent
SMS message sent

description.task: Demonstrate the use of interfaces for defining contracts — a key principle used in both Java EE and Spring. Build a simple payment system with multiple implementations behind a common interface.
instructions: Implement CreditCardProcessor: getProviderName() returns 'Credit Card', processPayment() prints a message and returns true.
Implement PayPalProcessor: getProviderName() returns 'PayPal', processPayment() prints a message and returns true.
Add CheckoutService with a constructor taking PaymentProcessor and a processOrder(double amount) method.
In Main, process an order of 250.0 with each processor.
description.hint: Programming to an interface allows you to swap implementations without changing the consumer code.
description.expectedOutput:
Processing $250.0 via Credit Card
Payment successful: true
Processing $250.0 via PayPal
Payment successful: true

description.task: Write a Java program that shows code duplication problems that Spring solves. Implement a 'before Spring' approach where you manually manage object creation vs a DI approach. Count lines of wiring code.
description.context: Before frameworks like Spring, developers had to manually wire all object dependencies, leading to boilerplate code and tight coupling.
instructions: In ManualWiring.setup(), create UserRepository, UserService (passing repo), and UserController (passing service), then call display().
In DIWiring.setup(), simply call ctrl.display() since dependencies are already injected.
In Main, demonstrate both approaches and note the difference.
Print a label before each approach.
description.hint: Manual wiring accumulates rapidly in large applications. DI containers like Spring handle this automatically at startup.
description.expectedOutput:
--- Manual Wiring ---
Users: [User1, User2]
--- DI Wiring ---
Users: [User1, User2]
